title: Surge credentials should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: Surge deployment credentials detected (email:token pair). Surge.sh is a static web publishing platform that uses email and authentication tokens for deployment operations. These credentials provide full deployment access to your Surge projects enabling: publish and update static websites to domains, overwrite existing deployments erasing production content, deploy malicious or defaced content to your domains, delete published projects and domains, claim and register new domains under your account, modify DNS records and domain configurations, access deployment history and project metadata, list all domains and projects in your account, consume deployment quota and bandwidth allocation, abuse infrastructure for hosting malicious content or phishing sites, damage brand reputation through unauthorized deployments, and potentially access custom domain configurations and SSL certificates. Exposed Surge credentials allow attackers to: deploy sophisticated phishing sites mimicking your brand on your domains, inject malicious JavaScript into existing websites for credential harvesting or cryptocurrency mining, replace legitimate production sites with defacement or propaganda, use your domains for malware distribution or command-and-control infrastructure, exhaust deployment limits preventing legitimate updates, steal traffic and SEO rankings by redirecting to malicious sites, access analytics data and visitor information from deployment logs, take over custom domains and SSL certificates, deploy fake login pages for social engineering attacks, compromise user trust by serving malicious content from trusted domains, manipulate search engine rankings for SEO attacks, host spam or illegal content destroying domain reputation, and create persistent backdoors in web applications. The security impact extends to: complete compromise of all Surge-hosted sites and domains, regulatory violations from hosting malicious content (phishing, malware distribution), legal liability for content served from your infrastructure, loss of domain reputation and SEO rankings requiring months to recover, breach of customer trust if customer-facing sites are compromised, potential data exposure if deployment scripts contain sensitive information, financial costs for cleanup, reputation restoration, and legal response, brand damage from defaced or compromised sites, business continuity impact from production site downtime, compliance violations for hosting regulated content (GDPR, CCPA, PCI DSS), email account compromise risk if same credentials used elsewhere, and incident response costs for forensics and security audits. Financial implications include: costs from malicious bandwidth consumption and resource abuse (Surge Pro plans have higher quotas attackers can abuse), domain reputation recovery requiring expensive SEO and brand restoration services, potential loss of custom domains if attackers transfer or delete them, legal fees from content served from your infrastructure, customer compensation if services are disrupted, cyber insurance deductibles and premium increases, emergency security response and forensic investigation costs, business interruption losses during site recovery, marketing expenses to rebuild brand trust, and potential regulatory fines for inadequate security controls. IMMEDIATE REMEDIATION REQUIRED: 1) URGENT: Revoke exposed Surge credentials immediately by logging into surge.sh dashboard or using 'surge logout' CLI command, then 'surge login' to create new session token; 2) If email password was exposed, change email account password immediately and enable MFA; 3) Audit all Surge deployments and domains: list all projects with 'surge list', verify each deployment is legitimate and unmodified, check deployment history for unauthorized changes, examine deployed content for injected malicious code or defacement, review domain configurations and DNS settings, verify SSL certificates haven't been tampered with; 4) Immediately redeploy all Surge projects from known-good source with new credentials to ensure clean state; 5) Rotate credentials for all associated accounts: if same email/password used for other services, change those immediately, update deployment scripts and CI/CD pipelines with new credentials, review email account access logs for suspicious activity, enable MFA on email account if not already active; 6) Security hardening: store Surge credentials ONLY in secure secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault, GCP Secret Manager), never commit Surge credentials to version control or configuration files, use environment variables injected at deployment time from secure sources, implement separate Surge accounts per project or environment (dev, staging, prod) with principle of least privilege, enable MFA on Surge account login where available, implement IP whitelisting for CI/CD deployments if Surge supports it, use dedicated deployment credentials separate from personal accounts, rotate Surge tokens monthly or quarterly as part of security policy, maintain secure inventory of all Surge accounts, domains, and associated credentials; 7) Implement deployment monitoring: enable alerts for all Surge deployment events, monitor deployment logs for unauthorized activity, implement webhook notifications for deployment changes, set up automated deployment verification comparing deployed content with source repository, establish baseline deployment patterns and alert on anomalies, monitor bandwidth and resource usage for abuse indicators, implement automated security scanning of deployed sites post-deployment, configure uptime monitoring with immediate alerts for site changes or downtime; 8) Code repository security: scan entire Git history for Surge credentials using git-secrets, truffleHog, or detect-secrets tools, if credentials found in Git history, rotate immediately and force-push cleaned history OR migrate to new repository, implement pre-commit hooks to prevent credential commits, enable GitHub/GitLab secret scanning with blocking policies, conduct full audit of deployment scripts and configuration files, review CI/CD pipeline secrets and ensure proper encryption, search for Surge credentials in backup archives, local developer machines, and documentation; 9) CI/CD pipeline security: store Surge credentials as encrypted secrets in CI/CD platforms (GitHub Actions secrets, GitLab CI/CD variables, Jenkins credentials, CircleCI contexts), implement deployment approval workflows requiring human authorization for production, use separate credentials for CI/CD with minimal necessary permissions, enable audit logging for all CI/CD secret access, implement secret rotation automation in deployment pipelines, restrict CI/CD secret access to specific branches (main, production), enable MFA or approval gates for critical deployments, document deployment procedures and credential handling in runbooks; 10) Domain and SSL security: review all custom domains associated with Surge account for unauthorized additions, verify DNS records haven't been modified to redirect traffic, check SSL/TLS certificates for unexpected changes or expirations, ensure domain registrar accounts are secure with strong passwords and MFA, implement domain locking to prevent unauthorized transfers, monitor domain reputation on security blacklists, configure CAA DNS records to restrict certificate issuance, enable DNSSEC where supported for domain integrity; 11) Content security: perform security audit of all deployed static sites for injected malicious code, scan deployed JavaScript files for cryptocurrency miners, backdoors, or data exfiltration code, review HTML for injected phishing forms or credential harvesting attempts, check for unauthorized redirects or modified links, implement Content Security Policy (CSP) headers on all deployments, use Subresource Integrity (SRI) for third-party scripts, enable HTTPS-only with HSTS headers, implement security headers (X-Content-Type-Options, X-Frame-Options, etc.); 12) Incident response: document this exposure in security incident log with full timeline, conduct post-incident review identifying how credentials were exposed and remediation measures, notify affected stakeholders if customer-facing sites were compromised, prepare breach notification if user data potentially exposed, review regulatory obligations (GDPR Article 33, CCPA §1798.82, etc.), engage legal counsel if malicious content was hosted, update incident response playbooks with Surge-specific procedures, conduct team training on credential security and deployment best practices; 13) Monitoring and detection: implement SIEM integration for Surge deployment events if available, set up alerts for: any deployment activity outside business hours, deployments from unknown IP addresses or geolocations, multiple failed login attempts or suspicious authentication patterns, new domain registrations or domain configuration changes, bandwidth usage spikes indicating resource abuse, website content changes detected by monitoring tools, SSL certificate modifications or expirations, DNS record changes for hosted domains; configure automated deployment notifications to security team, implement file integrity monitoring on deployed sites, establish baseline deployment frequency and size patterns; 14) Organizational controls: establish Surge security policy with approved usage guidelines, require manager approval for new Surge accounts or domains, implement quarterly access reviews of all Surge credentials and accounts, maintain centralized inventory of Surge sites, domains, and owners, conduct regular security training on static site deployment security, implement principle of least privilege for deployment access, establish credential rotation schedule with automated reminders, require use of corporate secrets managers for all Surge credentials, document all Surge accounts in IT asset inventory; 15) Long-term prevention: implement pre-commit hooks scanning for Surge credentials (git-secrets, talisman), use automated secret scanning in all repositories (GitHub Advanced Security, GitLab Secret Detection, Snyk), conduct regular penetration testing of deployment processes, implement just-in-time access for deployment credentials where possible, establish secure onboarding/offboarding procedures for handling Surge access, migrate to more secure deployment platforms with better credential management if possible, implement infrastructure-as-code for deployment configurations with secret references only, conduct quarterly security audits of all static site hosting and deployment processes, establish bug bounty program including deployment security, maintain security incident response procedures specific to static site compromises. CRITICAL REMINDERS: NEVER commit Surge credentials to Git repositories, configuration files, or documentation; NEVER use personal Surge accounts for production deployments (use dedicated service accounts); NEVER share Surge credentials via email, Slack, or unencrypted channels; NEVER reuse Surge credentials across multiple projects without careful risk assessment; ALWAYS store credentials in enterprise secrets managers with encryption at rest and in transit; ALWAYS implement MFA on email accounts associated with Surge for additional protection; ALWAYS use separate credentials per environment (development, staging, production); ALWAYS rotate credentials immediately upon employee departure or role change; ALWAYS audit deployment logs and monitor for unauthorized activity; ALWAYS implement least privilege access with project-specific accounts where possible. For additional security guidance: review Surge.sh documentation on authentication and security best practices, implement static site security headers and Content Security Policy, consider using Surge in combination with CI/CD security scanning and approval workflows, evaluate alternative static hosting platforms with enhanced security features (Netlify with role-based access, Vercel with team permissions, Cloudflare Pages with advanced controls), implement defense-in-depth with multiple security layers, and consult security professionals for architecture review of static site deployment infrastructure.

Surge deployment credentials detected (email and token in configuration format). Surge.sh uses email and UUID-format authentication tokens for static site deployment. These credentials provide complete access to deploy, update, delete websites and manage domains in your Surge account. Exposure enables unauthorized website deployments, content manipulation, domain hijacking, and infrastructure abuse. This allows attackers to: deploy malicious phishing sites on your domains, inject malware or cryptocurrency miners into existing sites, delete production deployments causing service outages, register and claim additional domains under your account, exhaust deployment quotas and bandwidth, damage brand reputation with defaced content, and host illegal content leading to legal liability. Take immediate action: revoke exposed credentials using 'surge logout' and 'surge login' CLI commands or through surge.sh dashboard, audit all deployments for unauthorized changes using 'surge list' and verify content integrity, rotate all associated credentials including email password if same credentials used elsewhere, implement secure credential storage using secrets managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, Azure Key Vault) never committing to Git, enable MFA on email account associated with Surge for additional security, use separate Surge accounts per environment (dev/staging/prod) with principle of least privilege, monitor deployment logs for suspicious activity and implement alerting for unauthorized changes, implement pre-commit hooks and automated secret scanning to prevent future exposures, review and secure CI/CD pipelines ensuring deployment credentials are stored as encrypted secrets with restricted access, conduct security audit of all deployed sites checking for injected malicious code or unauthorized modifications, and establish credential rotation policy with regular token refresh and proper documentation. Remember: Surge credentials should NEVER be committed to version control, included in configuration files tracked by Git, shared via email or messaging platforms, reused across multiple projects without risk assessment, or stored in plain text on developer machines or deployment servers. Always use environment variables loaded from secure secrets managers, implement deployment approval workflows for production, maintain audit logs of all deployment activities, and educate team on secure deployment practices and credential management.
